coredata.dc:title: A value product after the hydrothermal treatment of sludge: Carbon quantum dots and its application
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Chuanjin
Dong, Bin
Xu, Zuxin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are highly promising fluorescent nanomaterials with a wide range of applications. This study presented a novel, clean, hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) method for synthesizing fluorescent CQDs from sludge. A response surface design method was employed to optimize synthesis conditions, resulting in CQDs with excellent fluorescence properties. They were excited at 320 nm and emitted blue fluorescence at 450 nm under UV light. A mechanism for the formation of CQDs from sludge during HTC was proposed and revealed that the CQDs had an aromatic polymer structure with abundant oxygen-containing groups. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy confirmed N and Fe doping of CQDs, which significantly improved their fluorescence properties. Additionally, a fluorescence quenching analysis showed that the CQDs selectively detected Pb2+ in the aqueous phase, indicating their potential application in Pb2+ detection. The photoinduced electron transfer (PET) arising from amino groups and other reductive functional groups has been shown to align with the quenching mechanism during the detection of Pb2+ by CQDs. This study provided a cost-effective and scalable approach that could be used to produce CQDs for the in situ detection of Pb2+ in wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrothermal carbonization
Sludge-based CQDs
Fluorescence characteristics
Formation mechanism
Pb2 + sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Use of the RSM to optimize the QYs for CQDs produced by the HTC process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV-Vis absorption spectra of the CQDs and QSD. Inset: photograph of the CQDs and QSD solution under 320 nm excitation. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra for the CQDs solutions under different excitation wavelengths. (c) and (d) FT-IR and Raman spectra of the CQDs, respectively.
Proposed formation pathways for sludge-based CQD precursors during the HTC process (temperature = 160–240 oC, time = 5.02 h, and FeCl3 concentration = 5.77 g/L).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs at different concentrations of Pb2+ (0–4000 nM/L). (b) Linear relationship between (I-I
0
)/I
0 and Pb2+ concentration (nM/L=nmol/L).

(a) Radar plot showing the number of possible precursor-product pairs during fluorescence quenching of CQDs by Pb2+. (b) Van Krevelen diagrams of CQD composition variation during the quenching process. (c) The CQDs are quenched by Pb2+ via the PET quenching mechanism for CQDs.
Detection of Pb2+ in sewage plants and tap water.
Comparison of the developed fluorescent Pb2+ sensor with some previous sensors.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aLOD, limit of detection; bLCR, linear concentration range; cNA, not available.

head.ce:title: A value product after the hydrothermal treatment of sludge: Carbon quantum dots and its application

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Tongji University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are highly promising fluorescent nanomaterials with a wide range of applications. This study presented a novel, clean, hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) method for synthesizing fluorescent CQDs from sludge. A response surface design method was employed to optimize synthesis conditions, resulting in CQDs with excellent fluorescence properties. They were excited at 320 nm and emitted blue fluorescence at 450 nm under UV light. A mechanism for the formation of CQDs from sludge during HTC was proposed and revealed that the CQDs had an aromatic polymer structure with abundant oxygen-containing groups. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy confirmed N and Fe doping of CQDs, which significantly improved their fluorescence properties. Additionally, a fluorescence quenching analysis showed that the CQDs selectively detected Pb2+ in the aqueous phase, indicating their potential application in Pb2+ detection. The photoinduced electron transfer (PET) arising from amino groups and other reductive functional groups has been shown to align with the quenching mechanism during the detection of Pb2+ by CQDs. This study provided a cost-effective and scalable approach that could be used to produce CQDs for the in situ detection of Pb2+ in wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Municipal sludge is a combination of solid, semi-solid, and liquid waste materials generated by the municipal wastewater treatment process. It contains high concentrations of organic matter, nutrients (such as nitrogen and phosphorus), heavy metals, pathogenic bacteria, and pathogens [1]. The increasing capacities of sewage treatment plants has led to a significant rise in sludge production over recent years. Hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) has attracted significant attention from researchers as it can reduce, stabilize, and make sludge harmless, while consuming relatively little energy. If biodegradable organic substrates, such as proteins and carbohydrates, are viewed as potential carbon sources rather than waste, then sludge can become an attractive recyclable resource and a value-added product [2]. Previous studies have focused on the use of hydrochar (a solid product from HTC), whereas the treatment of hydrothermal liquids has been overlooked. These liquids cannot be directly discharged due to their high concentration of dissolved organic matter (DOM). These high concentrations pose a significant challenge when attempting to apply HTC technology at the industrial scale. Therefore, current research is focused on treating HTC fluids or discovering high-value products in these fluids, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [3].

The use of the CQDs quenching effect to determine heavy metal ion concentrations has shown considerable potential because it is a simple, rapid, sensitive, and selective means of detection. The CQDs are new fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with a particle size of less than 10 nm and a graphite crystal form or diamond-like structure. They are monodispersed spherical nanoparticles with carbon as the basic skeleton and are surface rich in oxygen-containing groups [3,4]. Hydrothermal CQDs exhibit controllable fluorescence emission properties without surface passivation. The raw materials used to produce CQDs by HTC can be pure compounds, such as glucose [5,6] and lignin [7,8], or more complex organic systems, such as pine wood [9], water chestnut, onion [10], pomelo juice [11,12], and rice residue [13]. The CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal synthesis using substances rich in amino acids and proteins as precursors can emit fluorescence without surface passivation and have good biocompatibility, which means that they are mostly used in biological imaging [14,15]. The quantum yields (QYs) of CQDs synthesized by the hydrothermal synthesis of carbohydrates and protein have been shown to be higher than those of CQDs synthesized with carbohydrates as a precursor [16,17]. In addition, doping with N and S atoms can effectively regulate the optical properties of CQDs and the fluorescence emitted under excitation at different wavelengths can effectively cover most areas of visible light [4,13,16,18]. The HTC conditions affect the formation of CQDs carbon nuclei and the composition of surface functional groups, which subsequently affect the particle size and optical properties of CQDs [14,19]. In addition, the crystallinity of hydrothermal CQDs is also affected by temperature and high temperature carbonization promotes the graphitization of CQDs, which leads to a redshift in the fluorescence emission peak. Furthermore, their size decreases, the graphitization degree and the fluorescence intensity increase, and the ultraviolet absorption peak widens with the extension of time [20].

Pollution from heavy metal ions, such as Pb2+ and Hg2+ in sewage, is a serious environmental issue across the globe. Several researchers have attempted to design simple and sensitive fluorescence methods using the attractive properties of CQDs. A variety of CQDs fluorescence detection methods for heavy metal ions, such as Hg2+, Pb2+ [21], Cd2+ [22], and Cr3+ [23], have been developed. These methods are usually based on the chelation of ions with functional groups on CQDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching (turning off) or enhancement (turning on). Low cost and sustainability are essential criteria when selecting carbohydrates as a carbon source to produce CQDs. The technology associated with the in-situ detection of wastewater from wastewater plants using CQDs prepared from sludge has a promising application potential. To date, there have been few reference studies on the production of CQDs by sludge HTC compared to biomass, especially the influence of HTC conditions (temperature, residence time, additives, etc.) on CQD characteristics. Moreover, only a limited number of studies have investigated the molecular transformation pathways and quenching mechanisms involved in the detection process.

In this study, water-soluble fluorescence CQDs were synthesized using sludge as the raw material by a one-pot method. The main components of sludge, such as polysaccharides, cellulose, and protein, were decomposed into glucose and amino acids by HTC, which meant that they were good precursors for the preparation of CQDs. The presence of amino functional groups in the protein enabled the effective N doping of prepared CQDs and resulted in excellent optical properties. Additionally, Fe3+, which is present at high concentrations in some types of sludge, could be doped into CQDs to improve the QY [24]. This study also proposed a conversion path for CQDs precursor substances by the sludge HTC process based on the results of a Fourier transform ion cyclotron resonance mass spectrometry (FTICR-MS) analysis. Furthermore, the QYs from CQDs was improved by modifying the hydrothermal conditions (temperature, residence time, and addition of FeCl3). The synthesized CQDs were used to detect heavy metal ions and the selectivity and sensitivity of CQDs detection of Pb2+ were analyzed. The molecular transformation path and a mechanism for the quenching process were also discussed. The exploration and enhancement of CQDs as an added value by-product of the sludge HTC process provided a valuable reference for sludge resource utilization.

The sludge sample used in this study was obtained from a sewage treatment plant located in Anhui, China. The sludge was stored at 4 °C, and contained 95% moisture before the experiment began. The FeCl3, CH3CH2OH, HCl, NaOH, and the solutions containing metals ions (Fe3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Ni2+, Hg2+, and Pb2+) were purchased from Sinopharm Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The UV/Vis absorption was measured using a DR 6000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan), the fluorescence spectra were measured using an Aqualog three-dimensional fluorescence synchronous absorption fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba, Kyoto, Japan), and Raman testing was performed using a Horiba HR Evolution Raman spectrograph. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out using a Thermo Scientific K-alpha instrument (Thermo Fisher, Waltham, MA, USA) and the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher). The micromorphologies and sizes of the CQDs hybrid products were characterized using a Jeol JEM-F200 transmission electron microscope (Jeol Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Negative ion mode electrospray ionization (ESI) combined with FT-ICR MS is often used to characterize the composition of DOM from various sources, including water, soil, and sediment. This powerful analytical approach can characterize almost all types of DOM components. Therefore, the conversion path for CQD precursors and the mechanism driving Pb2+ ion quenching of CQDs fluorescence were analyzed using FTICR-MS detection. More detailed information is provided in the Supplementary materials.

The CQDs were hydrothermally synthesized using sludge as a carbon source and Fe3+ as an additive. The synthesis procedure involved the addition of 20 g of sludge to a 100 mL Teflon reaction kettle containing 50 mL of Fe ion solution. The reaction mixture was then sealed and heated to the desired temperature. Following the reaction, the resultant mixture was subjected to centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min and the supernatant was subsequently filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane to eliminate any residual materials. The resultant yellowish aqueous dispersion of CQDs was further purified by dialysis using a cellulose membrane (1000 Da molecular weight cut-off) and ultra-pure water for 48 h. The purified CQDs solution was stored at 4 °C under light-protected conditions. The solid hydrochar products were washed sequentially with 100 mL of ultra-pure water and CH3CH2OH and then dried in an oven at 80 °C for subsequent use.

The response surface method (RSM) is a valuable tool that can be used to design experiments. It uses experimental data to estimate the correlation between input variables (experimental operation parameters) and outputs (responses). Based on the Box-Behnken central combination principle, the fluorescence yield of the CQDs was selected as the response value and three factors (temperature (X
1 ), reaction time (X
2 ), and FeCl3 concentration (X
3 )) were selected to optimize the technical conditions for sludge hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs. The experimental factors and levels are shown in Table S1. The obtained data were adjusted to a second-order polynomial regression model:
(1)

X
3
2

Fisher’s F-test (F-value), and P-value were used to evaluate the significance of the model and the effects of the variables, whereas the correlation coefficients (R
2
, R
adj
2 ) were used to evaluate the precision and feasibility of the second-order model.

The QYs of the CQDs were tested according to Qi et al. [13]. Quinine sulfate (QSD) (QY = 54% in 0.·05 M H2SO4 at 350 nm) was chosen to calculate the QY according to Eq. (2):
(2)
QY
1
=
QY
2
S
1
S
2
A
2
A
1
n
1
2
n
2
2

In Eq. (2), subscripts 1 and 2 denote the unknown substance to be measured and the reference, respectively, QY refers to the fluorescence quantum yield, S refers to the fluorescence peak area, A refers to the absorbance, and n refers to the refractive index of the solvent (The concentrations of the unknown substance to be measured and the reference substance in this experiment were extremely low so both were given a value of 1.33). The absorbance of the sample was controlled within a range of 0.05 to reduce the decrease in QY caused by the internal filtration effect.

The influence of different pHs (1−12) and UV irradiation duration (wavelength = 365 nm, 20–120 min) on fluorescence intensity were investigated to determine the stability of the CQDs. The CQDs for metal ions detection were selected by diluting different metal ions (Fe3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Ni2+, Hg2+, or Pb2+) in the CQDs solution. Then, the relationship between the concentration of selected metal ions and the fluorescence quenching effect was determined. In a typical run, 3 mL of CQDs solution and 3 mL of metal ion solution with a known concentration were mixed for 20 min. The fluorescence intensity was measured and the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and ion concentration was established. The above experiments were carried out at 25 °C room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Use of the RSM to optimize the QYs for CQDs produced by the HTC process
ce:section.ce:para:
The RSM was adopted to optimize the preparation conditions for CQDs and 17 groups of experimental schemes were obtained. Response variables for optimization were first identified: for hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs, temperature and holding time are the main influencing factors that determine the extent of hydrolysis and nucleation growth of sludge feedstock. In addition, based on the fact that iron flocculants would be used in the actual sludge, the CQDs QY is enhanced by controlling the Fe addition. The specific parameter ranges referred to conventional temperatures and holding times for sludge hydrothermal treatment [25]. The QY was the response value in the data analysis. The experimental schemes and QY results are shown in Table S2. After fitting a quadratic multinomial regression to the experimental results, a ternary quadratic regression equation (expressed by actual value) was obtained, as shown in Eq. (3):
(3) Y = –46·5704 + 0·2932X1 + 3·0846X2 + 2·6851X3 – 1·3809 × 10–3 X1X2 – 7·0303 × 10–3 X2X3 – 0·0674 X1X3 – 4·3245 × 1–−4 X1 2 – 0·2152X2 2 – 0·0811X3 2

Table S3 shows that the model was statistically significant with a Model F-value of 120.36. The probability that the Model F-value had significantly changed due to noise was only 0.01%, indicating that the model could be used to accurately predict the results of the experiment. Furthermore, the effects of a missing item were not significant, as the Prob > F value was 0.4711, which is greater than the 0.05 significance level. The Prob > F values were less than 0.05 for X
1 , X
2 , X
3 , X
1
X
2 , X
1
X
3 , X
1
2 , X
2
2 , and X
3
2 , which confirmed the statistical significance of these model terms. However, X
2
X
3 had a value greater than 0.10, which indicated that this model term was not significant. Fig. S1 indicates that there was a significant interaction between the 3D curves for X
1
X
2 and X
1
X
3, which both had steep curves and elliptical contours. In contrast, the 3D curve for X
2
X
3 was flat and the contour line was circular, indicating no significant interaction, which was consistent with the analysis of variance results. These results are consistent with previous studies [3] and highlight that HTC temperature has important effects on the QYs of CQDs made from sludge. The high determination coefficient (R2) of 0.9813 indicated that the model could explain 98.13% of the response variability. The adjusted R2 (adj-R2) of 0.9853 confirmed that the input variables were significant for the proposed values and was reasonably consistent with the R2 value. However, the predicted-R2 (pred-R2 = 0.9497) was considerably lower than R2, indicating that the model fitted random noise to some extent. Table S2 shows that the experimental results agreed well with the predicted results and the coefficient of variation (CV = 5.04%) was less than 10%, demonstrating the high accuracy and reliability of the model.

Elevated temperatures inhibit rapid nucleation, leading to a decrease in the size of CQDs. Within a specific temperature range, the QY of CQDs increased with rising hydrothermal temperature. Moreover, the graphitization degree of CQDs increased, reducing their size and enhancing fluorescence intensity with prolonged holding time at a consistent hydrothermal temperature. In this study, a holding time of 5 h had already achieved a high QY, and continuing to extend the retention time has little benefit in improving the QY. In this study, a holding time of 5 h had already yielded a high QY. Further extension of the holding time showed marginal benefits in improving the QY. Similarly, a high QY was achieved with a holding time of 5 h and FeCl3 concentration of 5.77 g/L. Continuing to increase the retention time and Fe dosage resulted in only minimal gains in enhancing the QY. Finally, the model predicted optimum conditions (temperature = 235.8 oC, time = 5.02 h, and FeCl3 concentration = 5.77 g/L) were confirmed by the actual results, with the actual QY of 8.89% differing only by 0.14% from the model prediction of 9.03%. The HTC conditions affect the formation of carbon nuclei and surface functional group composition of CQDs, which in turn affects their particle size and optical properties. Therefore, unless there was a large-scale process modification in the wastewater treatment plant, it was possible to obtain stable CQDs by controlling the hydrothermal synthesizer conditions. So, the optimal CQDs solution was used for subsequent testing to explore its application potential as a detection probe.

The optical properties of the CQDs were investigated using UV/Vis and fluorescence emission spectra. The absorption spectrum of the CQDs had a broad band between 250 and 600 nm ( Fig. 1 (a)), indicating the presence of π-π * and n-π * transitions, which can be attributed to the CC and CO bonds. Further optical properties of the CQDs are shown in Fig. 1 (a), where images of the CQDs solution were taken at a wavelength of 320 nm in both visible and UV light. The images appeared transparent in visible light, but a distinct blue light was emitted under UV light. Combined with the results in Fig. 1 (b), the optimal excitation wavelength of the CQDs was determined to be 320 nm with an emission wavelength of 425 nm in the blue region of the visible spectrum. Therefore, 320 nm was chosen as the excitation wavelength for subsequent experiments. The emission wavelengths of the CQDs showed significant red-shifts at excitation wavelengths between 300 and 400 nm, suggesting the presence of polar groups (-OH and -COOH) in the aromatic polymer structure of the CQDs. The electron leap to produce relaxation in polar solvents such as ultrapure water resulted in different fluorescence emission times at different excitation wavelengths [26].

The FTIR spectra shown in Fig. 1 (c) indicated that Fe was successfully doped into the CQDs, as evidenced by the Fe-O peak at 579 cm–1. The successful integration of Fe introduced extra electron holes onto the surface of the CQDs, which subsequently improved the QY. Additionally, a C-O stretching frequency at approximately 1015 cm–1 was observed, along with peaks at 1410 cm–1 (attributable to the presence of aromatic CC), 1630 cm–1 (associated with the stretching frequency of the aromatic carbonyl group CO), and bands at 2932 and 3460 cm–1, which are indicative of N-H and O-H stretching frequencies, respectively [27,28]. The Raman spectrum of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1 (d)) had two prominent bands, the G-band (G for graphite) and the D-band (D for defects). The D-band, which appeared around 1350 cm–1 and corresponded to the A1g symmetry of disordered graphite, revealed the presence of graphitic domains in the carbon nano-materials. The micromorphology and particle size distribution of the CQDs were evaluated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. S2 (a) shows that the CQDs were uniformly dispersed at lower resolutions and had a 0.2 nm lattice structure at higher resolutions, which corresponded to the graphene (100) plane [29]. Furthermore, the average diameter of 100 CQDs particles was 5.46 nm. The transmission electron microscopy image further confirmed that the CQDs were dispersed, mostly spherical, and showed minimal agglomeration. However, the FTIR analysis suggested that hydrogen bonds had formed between carboxyl groups on the CQDs surface, leading to an increase in the size of pure CQDs.

The chemical compositions of the CQDs were determined using XPS. Fig. S3 shows four main peaks, which are associated with C, N, O, and Fe, respectively. These peaks indicated that the CQDs were mainly composed of these four elements, with relative contents of C-52.31%, O-36.55%, 4.26%-N, and Fe-6.88%, respectively. The four elements were subjected to peak deconvolution to further explain their properties. In Fig. S3 (b), the high resolution spectrum of the C1s showed three main peaks at 284.1, 285.6, and 287.96 eV, which were associated with C-C (68.49%), C-O-C (22.6%), and O-CO (8.91%), respectively. The results showed that the CQDs were mainly composed of CC and were rich in C-O and C-OOH. The possible existence of oxygen-rich functional groups in CQDs was inferred from the CC structure and the FTIR spectra also confirmed this conclusion. The O1s spectrum in Figs. S3 (c) could be divided into two peaks, 532.11 and 533.22 eV, which corresponded to C-O (95.24%) and CO (4.76%), respectively. Fig. S3 (d) shows that the N1s had six main peaks at 399.0, 399.5, 399.9, 400.4, 401.6, and 402.3 eV, which indicated the presence of nitrogen atoms in the form of pyrrolic-N (5.84%), protein-N (10.22%), amine-N (12.04%), pyridinic-N (36.50%), nitrile-N (29.56%) and quaternary-N (5.84%), respectively. The results also showed that part of the Fe in the solution was doped into the CQDs and that the valence state had changed. The spectrum for Fe 2p in Fig. S3 (e) could be divided into four peaks: 710.36, 713.82, 720.95, and 725.31 eV, which correspond to Fe (Ⅱ) (73.43%) and Fe (Ⅲ) (26.57%). It could be inferred from the XPS results that the CQDs contained aromatic polymer structures, were rich in oxy groups, such as carboxyl, carbon, and oxy groups, and that pyrrole nitrogen was present at the edge of the molecules.

The formation of CQDs is commonly understood to occur in three distinct stages: precursor hydrolysis, nucleation and growth, and surface modification. A large number of researchers have suggested that both hydrophobic (-CH3, -CC-, -COOR, -R-Cl, -C6H6) and hydrophilic (-OH, -NH2, -COOH, -CO) precursors are present during the nucleation and growth phases. Elevated temperature and pressure prompted the hydrophobic precursors to accumulate within the interior of the structure, while the hydrophilic precursors remained exposed on the exterior [30]. This iterative process culminates in the full development of CQDs [18,25]. In the context of sludge, the macromolecular hydrolysis process has a dual role: it determines the types of synthetic precursors for CQDs and simultaneously influences the surface modification process [16].

A total of 20 reactions were subjected to a mass difference network analysis, a practical approach that is used correlate the precursor and product pairs to better understand DOM transformation in the sludge HTC process (Table S4). Fig. 2 shows a possible formation path for sludge-based CQD precursors based on the main components of sludge (carbohydrate, lignin, and protein). The deamination reaction (-NH and -NH3), the Maillard reaction (-C2H2O and -C2H2O2), and deoxygenation (-O, -CO2, and -CO) were the main reactions in each temperature range. The corresponding number of possible precursor-product pairs, which had the largest number of possible reactions during the 160–180 oC stage, are also shown. Schmidt et al. [31] reported that the terminal amino groups are easily transformed into carbonyl groups during the Strecker deamination reaction (–NH, -NH3 +O). The deoxygenation products correspond to various functional groups, such as -CO2 for carboxylic acids, -CO for ketones and aldehydes, and -H2O for alcohols [32]. Alternatively, the Maillard reaction (-C2H2O2) can give rise to glyoxal and these furfural and glyoxal compounds are enriched with carbonyl groups. Proteins are completely hydrolyzed into peptides and amino acids of various sizes. Apart from the conventional Maillard reaction, the reducing sugars and amino acids can also undergo additional transformations. For instance, amino acids might undergo further decomposition via decarboxylation and deamination (-CO2 and -NH2). In addition, the amino acid hydrolysates and small organic acids undergo decarbonization, polymerization, and aromatization to form n-heterocyclic intermediates and these processes are also accompanied by demethylation (-CH2). The heteroatoms introduced in this process are key to the subsequent surface modification of CQDs. The lignin macromolecular chains break down into glucose monomers and further into furan acid, furan alcohol, polyphenols, and other structures.

This study investigated the fluorescence stability of CQDs under different environmental conditions, such as light exposure, pH, and ionic strength. Fig. 3 shows that the fluorescence intensities of the CQDs significantly decreased upon exposure to UV light. Conversely, the fluorescence intensity was relatively stable within the pH range 4–8. It is noteworthy that CQDs exhibited maximum fluorescence intensity at the original pH of the hydrothermal liquid, and a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed when the pH was adjusted to 1–2 by HCl. These observations suggested that CQDs were remarkably stable under a wide range of pH conditions. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I/I
0 ) of the CQDs was measured after exposure to different metal ions. Among the tested metal ions, Pb2+ had the most significant fluorescence quenching effect on the CQDs, which indicated strong selectivity. The luminescence mechanism for hydrothermal CQDs was primarily attributed to the radiation recombination luminescence of excitons. During the formation of CQDs, energy potential wells were created on their surface. These trapped electrons and led to the emission of fluorescence via the e-/h+ radiation recombination. Therefore, UV irradiation or highly acidic/alkaline conditions caused functional groups, such as -NH2 and -COOH on the CQDs surface, to decompose, resulting in decreased defect states and inhibition of the non-radiative recombination process. This ultimately led to a reduction in fluorescence intensity.

The results indicated that the prepared CQDs selectively detected Pb2+ with little interference from other metal ions. The fluorescence intensity was measured by adding different concentrations of Pb2+ to determine the detection range for CQDs. Fig. 4 (a) shows that the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs gradually decreased when the Pb2+ concentration increased from 0 nM to 4000 nM/L. Fig. 4 (b) shows a good linear relationship between Pb2+ concentration and fluorescence intensity, where the linear relationship could be described by Y = 1.3628 × 10–3X + 0.01851 (0.018–90 nM/L, R2 = 0.9942) and Y = 3.8708 × 10-5X + 0.1478 (100–4000 nM/L, R2 = 0.9906). The detection limit for Pb2+ was estimated to be 0.018 nM/L.

A sludge-based fluorescence sensing platform has been developed and successfully used to detect Pb in real water samples, including sewage plant and tap water. The detection limits for the sewage plant and tap water samples were found to be 26.12 nM/L and 10.09 nM/L, respectively, which fell below the WHO guideline limit [33]. Recovery experiments were conducted using the standard addition method and the results, presented in Table 1, showed that the spiked sample recoveries in sewage plant water ranged from 95.8% to 110.10% with relative standard deviations (RSD) of 2.3–3.1%. Similarly, the recovery rates for tap water were estimated to be 98.80–103.80% with RSDs of 1.7–2.7%. In conclusion, the use of CQDs to detect Pb in real water samples was not only simple and cost-effective, but also highly sensitive and accurate. Therefore, the CQDs-based fluorescence sensing platform could be used to monitor Pb in environmental waters.

To better understand CQDs transformation in the quenching process, FTICR MS analyses were conducted on both the initial CQDs liquid and the resultant CQDs liquid after quenching induced by Pb2+ (4000 Nm/L). In addition, 20 reactions were subjected to a mass difference network analysis, a practical approach that is used to correlate the precursor and product pairs (Table S4). The radar map in Fig. 5 (a) shows that the deamination reaction (-NH and -NH3) and decarboxylation reaction (-CO2) were the main reactions in the quenching process and these process were accompanied by oxygen addition (amino groups are oxidized to aldehyde groups, etc.) and deoxygenation reactions (hydroxyl groups are oxidized to aldehyde groups, carboxyl groups, etc.), respectively. There were 924 precursor molecules (unique to CQDs) and 635 product molecules (unique to CQDs-Pb). Notably, the shared count of molecules both prior to and after quenching was 3209, underscoring the relatively modest proportion of molecules engaged in the quenching process. Fig. 5 (b) shows the Van Krevelen diagrams for CQDs composition variation during the quenching process. The findings indicated that proteins played a primary role in the quenching process and this could be attributed to their higher levels of reducing groups.

Photoinduced electron transfer (PET) can be explained by the occurrence of electron transfer between CQDs acting as either electron donors or receptors and the quencher functioning as either an electron receptor or donor. This transfer leads to the creation of cation and anion radicals, respectively. During this process, a complex is formed between the electron donor and receptor that is capable of returning to the ground state without emitting light. Photoinduced electron transfer can be categorized into reductive PET and oxidative PET. Reductive PET involves CQDs as electron receptors that accept electrons from electron donors, whereas oxidative PET is the opposite process. The driving force behind reductive electron transfer is the energy gap that exists between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) in the quencher and the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) of the CQDs [34]. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used to investigate the quenching mechanism for CQDs [35]. Fig. S5 shows that the onset of the reduction and oxidation potentials for CQDs, denoted as Ered and Eox, was − 0.52 eV and + 0.05 eV, respectively. Furthermore, the HOMO and LUMO values for CQDs were calculated as − 4.45 eV and − 3.88 eV, respectively, based on the empirical equations represented by Eq. 4 and Eq . 5 [36]. Because the LUMO for CQDs exceeded that of the electron acceptor Pb2+, an electron transfer from CQDs to Pb2+ was facilitated and this transfer aligns with oxidative PET. The Fe introduced via doping now functioned as an exceptional electron transfer medium, effectively accelerating the quenching process and enhancing the sensitivity of the detection method [24,37].
(4)
E
HOMO
= − e (
E
OX
+ 4.4 )
(5)
E
LUMO
= − e (
E
red
+ 4.4 )

In this study, the sludge was used as raw material, no expensive chemicals were added, and only water was used as the solvent. The whole production process can be a guide to the high-value utilization of sludge. The whole preparation process was simple, green and fully satisfied the requirements of clean production. Meanwhile, HTC technology is effective in sludge reduction and stabilization [38,39], therefore, the large-scale production of CQDs has a good economic basis. The low investment will produce high-value CQDs, which can be seen as a high-value by-product of the sludge treatment process. In the whole preparation process, Fe is doped into CQDs to improve the fluorescence yield, and part of it is also loaded on hydrochar after reduction, which makes the hydrochar has good magnetic properties and can be used as heavy metal adsorption material or mine remediation material [40,41]. Further, it can be considered to combine the sludge from waterworks with the sludge from sewage treatment plants for HTC. The iron-based flocculant used in waterworks can be used as the iron source to realize collaborative treatment and cyclization.

CQDs can emit bright blue light at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. However, the CQDs show weak fluorescence performance at other excitation wavelengths, low QY, and possible transfer of heavy metals from sludge into CQQs, which limits some other applications of CQQs, such as cell imaging. On the other hand, CQDs may show better application in the field of photocatalytic design due to their abundant oxygen-containing functional groups and excellent electron transfer ability on the surface. And the CQDs can also be used as fluorescent substrates for light emitting diode (LED) applications, and the excellent water solubility of hydrothermal CQDs gives them the ability to be processed in solution, facilitating the mixing of hydrothermal CQDs with other fluorescent substrates to produce multi-color light-emitting LEDs. In the next study, the effect of different components of sludge and different HTC conditions on the excitation wavelength of CQDs will be the focus of research, expecting to achieve self-regulation of excitation wavelength and increase quantum yield, and expand the application field of CQDs.

This study reported a simple synthesis method, based on a one-pot heat treatment method using sludge as a precursor, for CQDs that possessed excellent water solubility, fluorescence, and stability. In addition, a CQDs formation mechanism for the hydrothermal carbonization process was proposed that utilized the main components of the sludge. The CDQs had an aromatic polymer structure that was abundant in oxygen-containing functional groups and the as-synthesized CQDs were also successfully doped with N and Fe. The presence of reducing functional groups, such as N, classified the quenching mechanism as an oxidative PET process. In addition to creating additional vacancies within the structure of CQDs to enhance the fluorescence effect, the introduction of Fe doping also facilitated electron transfer within the quenching detection process. Furthermore, the prepared CQDs exhibited remarkable selectivity towards Pb2+ detection via fluorescence quenching, making them potentially promising Pb2+ fluorescent sensors with broad applicability in environmental monitoring. Importantly, this novel method significantly reduced the production cost of CQDs compared to other reported methods ( Table 2), which will facilitate their application in the large-scale preparation of Pb2+ fluorescent sensors or other functional materials.

Chuanjin Lin: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Bin Dong: Methodology, Conceptualization, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Zuxin Xu: Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Bin Dong reports financial support was provided by Bin. Bin Dong reports a relationship with Tongji University that includes: employment. Bin Dong has patent pending to Bin Dong. No other conflicts of interest.
